short_summary: The Dietary Inflammatory Index (DII) is a literature-derived tool designed to quantify the inflammatory potential of an individual's diet. Developed by systematically reviewing nearly 2,000 articles, it scores 45 dietary parameters based on their effects on key inflammatory biomarkers like C-reactive protein (CRP), interleukin-6 (IL-6), and tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α). This review covers the validation and application of the DII and its variants, such as the empirically-derived EDII and eADI, and population-specific versions like the CHINA-DII. Numerous studies have validated the DII, demonstrating that higher, pro-inflammatory scores are consistently associated with an increased risk of chronic diseases, including cardiovascular-kidney-metabolic syndrome, coronary artery disease, and certain cancers. The DII provides a standardized, evidence-based metric for researchers and clinicians to investigate diet-inflammation pathways and inform nutritional interventions for disease prevention and management.
long_summary: The Dietary Inflammatory Index (DII) is a quantitative, evidence-based tool designed to assess the inflammatory potential of an individual's diet. It was developed through a systematic review of 1,943 scientific articles, assigning inflammatory effect scores to 45 different food parameters based on their documented impact on six key biomarkers: CRP, IL-1β, IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, and TNF-α. This comprehensive review explores the DII's methodological evolution, including the development of an energy-adjusted version (E-DII) and the standardization of scores against a global reference database to enhance cross-population comparability. The article also details several adaptations of the DII framework. These include empirically derived indices like the Empirical Dietary Inflammatory Index (EDII) and the Empirical Anti-inflammatory Diet Index (eADI), which use statistical methods to identify dietary patterns most predictive of inflammatory markers in specific cohorts. Furthermore, population-specific versions like the CHINA-DII have been created to better reflect local dietary patterns. Extensive validation studies across diverse populations have consistently shown that higher, more pro-inflammatory DII scores are significantly associated with increased risk for numerous chronic conditions, including cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, frailty in the elderly, and various cancers. The DII's performance is also compared to other diet quality measures like the Healthy Eating Index (HEI-2015), providing a nuanced view of its specific utility in inflammation research.
